title: Arrhythmia — Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia
oneLiner: Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm with organised wide-complex tachycardia but no cardiac output; requires immediate defibrillation and ALS protocol.

sections.Arrhythmia — Pulseless Ventricular Tachy: **One-liner:** Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm with organised wide-complex tachycardia but no cardiac output; requires immediate defibrillation and ALS protocol.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Fine ventricular fibrillation (VF):** rapid, low-amplitude VF can mimic asystole on one lead. *Finding – check a second orthogonal lead; if any organised electrical activity, treat as shockable.*
- **Pulseless electrical activity (PEA):** organised electrical rhythm but no output – if mistaken for pVT you will delay giving adrenaline and treating reversible causes. *Finding – true pVT has a wide-complex rate >150/min; PEA usually has a slower, narrower QRS.*
sections.Presentation:
- Sudden collapse, unconscious, not breathing or agonal gasping.
- No pulse felt (carotid/femoral) – this is the defining feature.
- Monitor/defibrillator shows:
  - Wide QRS ≥0.12s (3 small squares)
  - Rate >150/min (often 150–300/min)
  - No P waves, atrioventricular dissociation may be absent on a single lead.
- Skin: mottled, cyanosed (seconds to minutes after arrest).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Ventricular fibrillation (VF):** disorganised, chaotic rhythm with no distinct QRS. *The only difference on ECG is regularity; if any doubt, treat as shockable.*
- **Asystole:** flatline; no electrical activity at all. *Differentiator – with asystole the line is straight or has only P waves; pVT shows clear QRS complexes.*
- **PEA:** organised rhythm (normal or slow rate) without a pulse. *Splitter – wide QRS favours pVT; narrow QRS favours PEA. Also, pulse check is definitive.*
- **Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) with aberrancy (rarely pulseless):** pre-existing bundle branch block or very fast rate can mimic VT. *Splitter – if any doubt, treat as pVT; do not delay defibrillation.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside** (do not delay defibrillation):
  - Confirm pulselessness: carotid or femoral pulse check (no more than 10 seconds).
  - Attach defibrillator pads – anterior-lateral (right clavicle, left axilla).
  - 3-lead ECG – look at lead II and aVR to confirm wide QRS.
  - Check for signs of life (movement, breathing) simultaneously.
  - If ROSC achieved: 12-lead ECG, blood glucose, and continuous monitoring.
- **Bloods (post-ROSC):**
  - Arterial blood gas (lactate, pH, K+, Ca2+).
  - Electrolytes (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+), troponin, full blood count, clotting.
- **Imaging (post-ROSC):**
  - Chest X-ray – line/ETT position, pneumothorax, cardiac silhouette.
  - Bedside echo (FoCUS) – to assess wall motion, effusion, LV function.
- **Never-miss test:** **12-lead ECG after ROSC** – identifies STEMI, baseline QT interval, ischaemia, or underlying cardiomyopathy. Omission may miss the reversible cause and risk recurrence.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / cardiac arrest algorithm)**
  - Follow ALS protocol (Resuscitation Council UK):
    1. **Call for help** – shout for AED/defibrillator, summon crash team.
    2. **Start CPR** – 30:2 at 100–120 compressions/min.
    3. **Defibrillate** – biphasic 150–200 J (or manufacturer spec).
       - Immediately resume CPR for 2 min after each shock.
    4. **After 3 shocks** – give **adrenaline 1 mg IV** (every 3–5 min).
    5. **After 3 shocks** (if still in pVT/VF) – give **amiodarone 300 mg IV** (bolus). Repeat at 150 mg after 5 shocks.
    6. Consider **magnesium sulphate 2 g IV** if torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with long QT).
  - Treat reversible causes: 4Hs and 4Ts (hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo-/hyperkalaemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis – coronary or pulmonary).
  - If no immediate defibrillator available – **don’t wait for rhythm confirmation**; start CPR and apply AED as soon as possible.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h)**
  - Post-ROSC care: target SpO2 94–98%, MAP ≥65 mmHg, normocapnia (PaCO2 4.5–5.5 kPa).
  - Treat underlying cause (primary PCI if STEMI, revascularisation, ICD if indicated).
  - Start antiarrhythmic maintenance therapy (e.g. amiodarone oral load 200 mg TDS for 1 week, then 200 mg OD – but seek cardiology advice).
  - Consider targeted temperature management (cooling to 32–36°C for 24 h) if unresponsive after ROSC.
- **Long-term / outpatient**
  - Cardiologist referral for ICD implantation (secondary prevention) if no reversible cause.
  - Optimise heart failure therapy (ACEi, beta-blocker, MRA) if LV dysfunction.
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 8 h if hypotensive (or as per local protocol); avoid excessive fluid if LV failure.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg subcut OD) unless bleeding risk or therapeutic anticoagulation.
  - **Diet/NBM:** initially NBM until airway secure and sedation weaned; then start on enteral feeding if swallowing safe.
  - **Observations:** continuous cardiac monitoring (telemetry) for at least 72 h; NEWS2 chart every 30 min initially, then hourly; record rhythm strips every 4 h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Amiodarone IV (cardiac arrest):** 300 mg IV bolus (into large vein or IO). Repeat 150 mg after 5 shocks. *Route: IV/IO push, then flush 10 mL 0.9% NaCl.*
- **Adrenaline IV (cardiac arrest):** 1 mg IV every 3–5 min.
- **Magnesium sulphate IV (torsades):** 2 g in 10 mL 0.9% NaCl over 1–2 min in cardiac arrest; post-ROSC: 2–4 g in 250 mL D5W over 1–4 h.
- **Lidocaine (alternative if amiodarone not available):** 1 mg/kg IV bolus, then 1–4 mg/min infusion. *First-line in some settings, but Resuscitation Council UK prefers amiodarone.*
- **Key contraindications:** amiodarone – avoid with other QT-prolonging drugs (e.g. certain antipsychotics, class IA antiarrhythmics). Lidocaine – caution in severe heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** none for single arrest doses. Post-ROSC amiodarone infusion – monitor for bradycardia and hypotension; reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving amiodarone as a slow infusion in cardiac arrest** – in arrest it must be given as a push (fast bolus). Also, **forgetting to flush after amiodarone** (phlebitis risk).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Recurrent arrest (refractory pVT/VF).
  - Post-arrest myocardial stunning and cardiogenic shock.
  - Anoxic brain injury (if ROSC delayed >10 min).
  - Airway and ventilation problems (ETT misplacement, pneumothorax from CPR).
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurological disability (cognitive, motor deficits).
  - ICD-related complications (infection, inappropriate shocks).
  - Drug-related (amiodarone: thyroid, liver, pulmonary fibrosis – with long-term use).
sections.Prognosis:
- Survival to hospital discharge after pVT/VF arrest is ~25% overall, but >50% if witnessed with bystander CPR and early defibrillaion. ROSC within 20 min and return of pupillary light reflex are favourable signs.
- Poor prognostic indicators: unwitnessed arrest, initial asystole, no ROSC after 30 min, absent brainstem reflexes.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **During CPR, charge the defibrillator while the last compression is given** – this minimises hands-off time.
- **Use the "LOVE" mnemonic for reversible causes:** Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hyper-/Hypokalaemia, Hypothermia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis.
- **After ROSC, cool the patient** – targeted temperature management improves neurological outcomes in comatose survivors.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Delaying defibrillation to "analyse" rhythm** – in suspected pVT, shock immediately; even if it’s fine VF, it’s still shockable.
- **Assuming pulselessness without checking carotid/femoral** – a faint pulse can be present in some wide-complex tachycardias (e.g. SVT). Check for 10 seconds only.
- **Giving adrenaline before defibrillation** – defibrillation first is proven to improve outcomes; adrenaline is given after the third shock.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- *Rare – most pVT patients are either admitted or die. If a very low-risk event (e.g. ICD shock terminated) and no acute ischaemia, discharge may occur. For these:*
- **Return if:** palpitations with presyncope, any loss of consciousness, chest pain, or ICD shock again.
- **Follow-up:** urgent cardiology clinic within 2 weeks; device interrogation as per local protocol.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call crash team (2222) immediately** – any pulseless arrhythmia is a cardiac arrest.
- **After ROSC:**
  - Call ICU/HDU for post-arrest care (especially if GCS <9 or haemodynamically unstable).
  - Call cardiology registrar for STEMI activation if indicated.
  - Use **NEWS2** – if ≥7, escalate to critical care outreach.
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** and **pH <7.2** after ROSC – consider sepsis/ischaemia and escalate.
  - **GCS ≤8** or no eye opening – consider therapeutic hypothermia and discuss with ICU.

rawMarkdown:
## Arrhythmia — Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT)

**One-liner:** Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm with organised wide-complex tachycardia but no cardiac output; requires immediate defibrillation and ALS protocol.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Fine ventricular fibrillation (VF):** rapid, low-amplitude VF can mimic asystole on one lead. *Finding – check a second orthogonal lead; if any organised electrical activity, treat as shockable.*
- **Pulseless electrical activity (PEA):** organised electrical rhythm but no output – if mistaken for pVT you will delay giving adrenaline and treating reversible causes. *Finding – true pVT has a wide-complex rate >150/min; PEA usually has a slower, narrower QRS.*

### Presentation
- Sudden collapse, unconscious, not breathing or agonal gasping.
- No pulse felt (carotid/femoral) – this is the defining feature.
- Monitor/defibrillator shows:
  - Wide QRS ≥0.12s (3 small squares)
  - Rate >150/min (often 150–300/min)
  - No P waves, atrioventricular dissociation may be absent on a single lead.
- Skin: mottled, cyanosed (seconds to minutes after arrest).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Ventricular fibrillation (VF):** disorganised, chaotic rhythm with no distinct QRS. *The only difference on ECG is regularity; if any doubt, treat as shockable.*
- **Asystole:** flatline; no electrical activity at all. *Differentiator – with asystole the line is straight or has only P waves; pVT shows clear QRS complexes.*
- **PEA:** organised rhythm (normal or slow rate) without a pulse. *Splitter – wide QRS favours pVT; narrow QRS favours PEA. Also, pulse check is definitive.*
- **Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) with aberrancy (rarely pulseless):** pre-existing bundle branch block or very fast rate can mimic VT. *Splitter – if any doubt, treat as pVT; do not delay defibrillation.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside** (do not delay defibrillation):
  - Confirm pulselessness: carotid or femoral pulse check (no more than 10 seconds).
  - Attach defibrillator pads – anterior-lateral (right clavicle, left axilla).
  - 3-lead ECG – look at lead II and aVR to confirm wide QRS.
  - Check for signs of life (movement, breathing) simultaneously.
  - If ROSC achieved: 12-lead ECG, blood glucose, and continuous monitoring.
- **Bloods (post-ROSC):**
  - Arterial blood gas (lactate, pH, K+, Ca2+).
  - Electrolytes (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+), troponin, full blood count, clotting.
- **Imaging (post-ROSC):**
  - Chest X-ray – line/ETT position, pneumothorax, cardiac silhouette.
  - Bedside echo (FoCUS) – to assess wall motion, effusion, LV function.
- **Never-miss test:** **12-lead ECG after ROSC** – identifies STEMI, baseline QT interval, ischaemia, or underlying cardiomyopathy. Omission may miss the reversible cause and risk recurrence.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / cardiac arrest algorithm)**
  - Follow ALS protocol (Resuscitation Council UK):
    1. **Call for help** – shout for AED/defibrillator, summon crash team.
    2. **Start CPR** – 30:2 at 100–120 compressions/min.
    3. **Defibrillate** – biphasic 150–200 J (or manufacturer spec).
       - Immediately resume CPR for 2 min after each shock.
    4. **After 3 shocks** – give **adrenaline 1 mg IV** (every 3–5 min).
    5. **After 3 shocks** (if still in pVT/VF) – give **amiodarone 300 mg IV** (bolus). Repeat at 150 mg after 5 shocks.
    6. Consider **magnesium sulphate 2 g IV** if torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with long QT).
  - Treat reversible causes: 4Hs and 4Ts (hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo-/hyperkalaemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis – coronary or pulmonary).
  - If no immediate defibrillator available – **don’t wait for rhythm confirmation**; start CPR and apply AED as soon as possible.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h)**
  - Post-ROSC care: target SpO2 94–98%, MAP ≥65 mmHg, normocapnia (PaCO2 4.5–5.5 kPa).
  - Treat underlying cause (primary PCI if STEMI, revascularisation, ICD if indicated).
  - Start antiarrhythmic maintenance therapy (e.g. amiodarone oral load 200 mg TDS for 1 week, then 200 mg OD – but seek cardiology advice).
  - Consider targeted temperature management (cooling to 32–36°C for 24 h) if unresponsive after ROSC.
- **Long-term / outpatient**
  - Cardiologist referral for ICD implantation (secondary prevention) if no reversible cause.
  - Optimise heart failure therapy (ACEi, beta-blocker, MRA) if LV dysfunction.
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 8 h if hypotensive (or as per local protocol); avoid excessive fluid if LV failure.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg subcut OD) unless bleeding risk or therapeutic anticoagulation.
  - **Diet/NBM:** initially NBM until airway secure and sedation weaned; then start on enteral feeding if swallowing safe.
  - **Observations:** continuous cardiac monitoring (telemetry) for at least 72 h; NEWS2 chart every 30 min initially, then hourly; record rhythm strips every 4 h.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Amiodarone IV (cardiac arrest):** 300 mg IV bolus (into large vein or IO). Repeat 150 mg after 5 shocks. *Route: IV/IO push, then flush 10 mL 0.9% NaCl.*
- **Adrenaline IV (cardiac arrest):** 1 mg IV every 3–5 min.
- **Magnesium sulphate IV (torsades):** 2 g in 10 mL 0.9% NaCl over 1–2 min in cardiac arrest; post-ROSC: 2–4 g in 250 mL D5W over 1–4 h.
- **Lidocaine (alternative if amiodarone not available):** 1 mg/kg IV bolus, then 1–4 mg/min infusion. *First-line in some settings, but Resuscitation Council UK prefers amiodarone.*
- **Key contraindications:** amiodarone – avoid with other QT-prolonging drugs (e.g. certain antipsychotics, class IA antiarrhythmics). Lidocaine – caution in severe heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** none for single arrest doses. Post-ROSC amiodarone infusion – monitor for bradycardia and hypotension; reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving amiodarone as a slow infusion in cardiac arrest** – in arrest it must be given as a push (fast bolus). Also, **forgetting to flush after amiodarone** (phlebitis risk).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Recurrent arrest (refractory pVT/VF).
  - Post-arrest myocardial stunning and cardiogenic shock.
  - Anoxic brain injury (if ROSC delayed >10 min).
  - Airway and ventilation problems (ETT misplacement, pneumothorax from CPR).
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurological disability (cognitive, motor deficits).
  - ICD-related complications (infection, inappropriate shocks).
  - Drug-related (amiodarone: thyroid, liver, pulmonary fibrosis – with long-term use).

### Prognosis
- Survival to hospital discharge after pVT/VF arrest is ~25% overall, but >50% if witnessed with bystander CPR and early defibrillaion. ROSC within 20 min and return of pupillary light reflex are favourable signs.
- Poor prognostic indicators: unwitnessed arrest, initial asystole, no ROSC after 30 min, absent brainstem reflexes.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **During CPR, charge the defibrillator while the last compression is given** – this minimises hands-off time.
- **Use the "LOVE" mnemonic for reversible causes:** Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hyper-/Hypokalaemia, Hypothermia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis.
- **After ROSC, cool the patient** – targeted temperature management improves neurological outcomes in comatose survivors.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Delaying defibrillation to "analyse" rhythm** – in suspected pVT, shock immediately; even if it’s fine VF, it’s still shockable.
- **Assuming pulselessness without checking carotid/femoral** – a faint pulse can be present in some wide-complex tachycardias (e.g. SVT). Check for 10 seconds only.
- **Giving adrenaline before defibrillation** – defibrillation first is proven to improve outcomes; adrenaline is given after the third shock.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- *Rare – most pVT patients are either admitted or die. If a very low-risk event (e.g. ICD shock terminated) and no acute ischaemia, discharge may occur. For these:*
- **Return if:** palpitations with presyncope, any loss of consciousness, chest pain, or ICD shock again.
- **Follow-up:** urgent cardiology clinic within 2 weeks; device interrogation as per local protocol.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call crash team (2222) immediately** – any pulseless arrhythmia is a cardiac arrest.
- **After ROSC:**
  - Call ICU/HDU for post-arrest care (especially if GCS <9 or haemodynamically unstable).
  - Call cardiology registrar for STEMI activation if indicated.
  - Use **NEWS2** – if ≥7, escalate to critical care outreach.
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** and **pH <7.2** after ROSC – consider sepsis/ischaemia and escalate.
  - **GCS ≤8** or no eye opening – consider therapeutic hypothermia and discuss with ICU.
